5 min read
2 hours ago

--

A real story about chaos, lessons, and finally finding a system that works

I remember the first full stack project I built on my own.
It was supposed to be a simple task management app. React on the front, Node.js and Express on the back, MongoDB for the database. Nothing fancy. I’d done tutorials. I’d watched videos. I felt ready.
Three weeks later, my project folder looked like a crime scene.
Files everywhere. API routes scattered across five different files with no logic to why. My React components were doing things components should never do — fetching data, handling business logic, managing global state, all at the same time. My .env file had secrets I'd accidentally pushed to GitHub twice.
I finished the project. It worked. But I could never go back and touch it without breaking something.
That experience taught me something important: knowing how to code and knowing how to structure a project are two completely different skills.

The Second Attempt Wasn’t Much Better
A few months later, I started working on a new project — a SaaS tool I was building with a small team.
I told myself I’d do it right this time. I even Googled “best folder structure for full stack apps” and picked the one with the most upvotes on Stack Overflow.
Big mistake.
I was following someone else’s structure without understanding why they made those decisions. So the moment my project grew beyond what the tutorial assumed, I was lost again. I started creating folders called misc and helpers and other-stuff. Never a good sign.
The project worked. But scaling it was painful. Adding a new feature meant touching six different files across four different folders. My teammates were confused every time they opened the codebase.
I still remember one of them messaging me: “Bro where does the auth logic actually live?”
I didn’t have a clean answer. That hurt.

What I Do Now — And Why It Actually Works
After two painful lessons, I developed a structure that finally made sense to me. Not because I read it somewhere — but because I understood the reasoning behind every decision.
Here’s how I structure a full stack project today.

The Root Level
my-project/
├── client/ # Everything React lives here
├── server/ # Everything Node.js lives here
├── .env.example # Template for environment variables
├── .gitignore
├── README.md
└── docker-compose.yml # If I'm containerizingSimple. Clean. Anyone who opens this repo immediately knows where to go.
The biggest mistake beginners make is mixing client and server files at the root level. Keep them completely separate from day one — even if it feels overkill for a small project.

The Client Side (React)
client/
├── public/
├── src/
│ ├── api/ # All API call functions live here
│ ├── components/ # Reusable UI components only
│ ├── pages/ # One file per page/route
│ ├── hooks/ # Custom React hooks
│ ├── context/ # Global state (Context API)
│ ├── utils/ # Helper functions
│ ├── styles/ # CSS or Tailwind config
│ └── App.jsx
└── package.jsonThe rule I follow: components should only care about UI. If a component is doing an API call directly, something is wrong. All API calls go into the api/ folder as clean functions. Components just call those functions.
This one rule alone made my React code 10x easier to read.

The Server Side (Node.js + Express)
server/
├── config/ # DB connection, environment config
├── controllers/ # Business logic lives here
├── middleware/ # Auth, error handling, validation
├── models/ # MongoDB/PostgreSQL schemas
├── routes/ # Route definitions only
├── utils/ # Reusable server-side helpers
├── app.js # Express app setup
└── server.js # Entry point, starts the serverThe key separation here is routes vs controllers.
Routes just define the endpoint and point to a controller:
// routes/userRoutes.js
router.get('/profile', authMiddleware, userController.getProfile);Controllers handle the actual logic:
// controllers/userController.js
export const getProfile = async (req, res) => {
try {
const user = await User.findById(req.user.id);
res.json(user);
} catch (error) {
res.status(500).json({ message: 'Server error' });
}
};When routes and logic are mixed together, your route files become 500-line monsters that nobody wants to open. Trust me — I’ve been there.

The Environment Variable System
This is the one thing most tutorials completely skip.
I keep three things:

• .env — actual secrets, never pushed to GitHub (in .gitignore)

• .env.example — a template showing what variables are needed (pushed to GitHub)

• A config/ folder that imports and validates all env variables in one place
// config/env.js
import dotenv from 'dotenv';
dotenv.config();export const config = {
mongoUri: process.env.MONGO_URI,
jwtSecret: process.env.JWT_SECRET,
port: process.env.PORT || 5000,
};Now instead of writing process.env.MONGO_URI scattered across 12 files, I import config.mongoUri from one place. If the variable name ever changes, I update it in exactly one file.

Bonus — The Database Layer
One more thing I added after my second project disaster: a dedicated models/ folder with clean, well-defined schemas.
// models/User.js
import mongoose from 'mongoose';const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
name: { type: String, required: true },
email: { type: String, required: true, unique: true },
password: { type: String, required: true },
createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now },
});export default mongoose.model('User', userSchema);Never define your schema inside a route or controller. Keep it clean, keep it separate.

The Three Rules I Never Break
After building multiple projects with this structure, I’ve boiled it down to three non-negotiable rules:
Rule 1: Decide the structure before writing a single line of code. Even 20 minutes of planning saves 20 hours of refactoring later.
Rule 2: If you can’t explain why a file is in a folder, move it. Every file should have an obvious home. If you’re creating a misc folder, something has gone wrong.
Rule 3: Structure for your future teammate — even if that teammate is just future you. Come back to your own code after 3 months and you’ll be grateful you did this.

Final Thought
Nobody gets project structure right on the first try. I definitely didn’t. But the good news is that you only need to get burned a couple of times before the right patterns start to feel obvious.
The structure I use now isn’t perfect — no structure is. But it’s consistent, and consistency is what actually matters when a project grows.
If you’re starting a new full stack project today, steal this structure. Adapt it. Make it yours.
And when you eventually find a better way — write about it. That’s how we all get better.

I’m Raj Namdev — Full Stack Developer with 5+ years of building real products. I write about things I’ve actually built, broken, and learned from. Follow me for more honest dev content.
